title: Spectral theory for ASEP, XXZ and the (q,mu,nu)-Boson process
speaker: Ivan Corwin

proof_stages.label: KPZ replica and delta-Bose-gas motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from the KPZ/stochastic-heat equation, explains that joint moments solve the attractive Lieb-Liniger delta Bose gas and can be attacked by Bethe ansatz plus Plancherel theory, and isolates the replica-trick difficulty caused by superexponential moment growth.

proof_stages.label: Discrete integrable-probability program and q-TASEP formula
proof_stages.summary: The speaker proposes replacing the ill-posed continuum replica reconstruction by discrete integrable systems whose observables have autonomous Bethe-solvable moment equations, then presents q-TASEP with step initial data and its nested-contour joint moment formula.

proof_stages.label: q-Boson duality and coordinate Bethe verification
proof_stages.summary: The q-Boson zero-range process is introduced as the discrete delta-Bose-gas dual to q-TASEP, and its free evolution plus two-body boundary conditions are used to verify the q-TASEP contour formula, including the step-initial-data residues.

proof_stages.label: ASEP parallel and unification problem
proof_stages.summary: ASEP is presented with an analogous current observable and contour formula, obtained through self-duality and coordinate Bethe ansatz, leading to the question of why ASEP and q-TASEP have such similar formulas.

proof_stages.label: q-Hahn lift of TASEP and Boson systems
proof_stages.summary: The q-Hahn distribution is used to define discrete-time q-Hahn TASEP and q-Hahn Boson processes, their degenerations are placed in context, and a duality theorem gives the corresponding autonomous q-Hahn Boson evolution and moment formula.

proof_stages.label: Bethe eigenfunctions for the q-Hahn Boson process
proof_stages.summary: To diagonalize the q-Hahn Boson evolution, the speaker introduces PT invariance, recalls the coordinate Bethe ansatz mechanism, derives the q-Hahn free evolution and boundary conditions, and writes the left and right Bethe eigenfunctions and eigenvalues.

proof_stages.label: q-Hahn Plancherel inversion theorem
proof_stages.summary: Direct and inverse transforms built from the q-Hahn Bethe eigenfunctions are defined, their contour Plancherel measure is described, and the main spectral theorem states that the transforms are mutual inverses, isometric, and yield biorthogonality.

proof_stages.label: Applications to initial data, ASEP, and XXZ
proof_stages.summary: The Plancherel theorem is applied to write moment formulas for more general initial data, to explain Tracy-Widom ASEP identities and their q-Hahn lift, and via a similarity transform to obtain Plancherel theory for the ferromagnetic XXZ chain, with remaining asymptotic questions emphasized.

examples.name: The k=1 stochastic-heat moment
examples.note: The speaker notes that for one spatial point the noise averages out and the first moment simply solves the heat equation, making the moment-to-Bose-gas correspondence concrete in the easiest case.
examples.engagement_reason: he specializes the stochastic heat equation moment equation to k=1 and explains why taking expectation kills the white noise and leaves the heat equation

passes.reason: Minimal noninteracting moment case where the stochastic term drops out.
It is the minimal k=1 moment, where the noise interaction drops out to the ordinary heat equation.

k=1 is the minimal noninteracting moment where the white-noise/delta interaction disappears, used only as an explanatory base case.
The k=1 stochastic-heat moment is the minimal noninteracting base case of the moment hierarchy, used only as an illustration.

examples.name: Single-particle Laplacian and Fourier transform
examples.note: The one-particle Laplacian, whose eigenfunctions are exponentials but whose L2 decomposition uses only the imaginary axis with the Fourier measure, is used to reveal why a Plancherel theory is needed for Bethe eigenfunctions.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses the one-particle Laplacian/Fourier transform as a familiar Plancherel analogy, stating the eigenfunctions and measure without deriving them

passes.reason: One-particle spectral base case with no many-body Bethe interaction.
The one-particle sector is the minimal noninteracting Plancherel example, reducing to ordinary Fourier analysis.
the one-particle free case is the minimal sector and is used to refute the naive all-complex-z decomposition
the one-particle free Laplacian is the minimal translation-invariant case where Plancherel theory reduces to ordinary Fourier analysis.
The single-particle Laplacian/Fourier transform is the minimal one-body Plancherel case before genuine many-body interactions appear.
the single-particle Laplacian is the one-body base case with ordinary Fourier rather than many-body Plancherel structure
It is the one-particle/free endpoint of the many-body spectral problem, with the highly translation-invariant Laplacian diagonalized by Fourier modes.
It is a one-particle free case used to illustrate Plancherel/Fourier decomposition before the interacting Bethe-ansatz setting.
the one-particle Laplacian/Fourier transform is used as the simplest Plancherel model before the interacting many-particle Bethe ansatz setting
free one-particle Laplacian is the translation-invariant base case for Fourier/Plancherel decomposition
The single-particle Laplacian is used as the simplest noninteracting Plancherel/Fourier base case rather than as an adversarial example.
the free one-particle Laplacian is the minimal translation-invariant case and shows that a naive all-complex-z spectral decomposition fails.

examples.name: Three-particle q-Boson cluster
examples.note: A cluster of three coincident q-Boson particles is used to show visibly how the two-body boundary conditions combine into the cluster jump rate via (1-q)(1+q+q^2)=1-q^3.
examples.engagement_reason: he analyzes the three-particle q-Boson cluster via the boundary conditions, rewrites derivatives with q-factors, and checks the rate becomes 1-q^3

passes.reason: Collapsed collision configuration with all three q-Boson particles in one cluster.
All three q-Bosons occupy one site, a collapsed diagonal and maximal cluster with full coordinate-permutation regularity.
the n1=n2=n3 configuration is a collapsed boundary stratum of the ordered particle chamber
the three-particle cluster lies on a collision boundary with collapsed coordinates, illustrating the boundary-condition mechanism rather than refuting anything.
The three-particle q-Boson cluster lies on the collapsed collision diagonal where several particle coordinates coincide.
the three-particle q-Boson cluster is a collapsed collision-boundary configuration with all coordinates equal
All three q-Boson coordinates coincide, giving a collision-boundary and maximally clustered configuration used to check the boundary condition.
The three particles are collapsed into one cluster on the boundary diagonal, used to verify the boundary-condition reduction.

all three q-Boson particles occupy one site, giving a collapsed collision cluster
The three-particle q-Boson cluster is the collapsed boundary case n1=n2=n3, maximal clustering for three particles.
the three particles are collapsed at one site on a boundary stratum, used to verify the q-Boson boundary condition.

examples.name: Multiparticle hopping asymmetric diffusion degeneration
examples.note: This continuous-time degeneration, including the simple q-to-1 rate 1/j and q-to-infinity equal-rate limits, illustrates the range of stochastic models contained in the q-Hahn family without being used in the main proof.
examples.engagement_reason: he takes a concrete degeneration of the q-Hahn process to the multiparticle hopping asymmetric diffusion process and computes/describes the resulting jump rates and their q-limits

passes.reason: Continuous-time parameter-scaling limit of the higher q-Hahn process.

examples.name: Six-vertex transfer-matrix analogy
examples.note: The commuting q-Hahn Boson transition matrices are compared with commuting transfer matrices in lattice models such as the six-vertex model, clarifying the role of the parameter nu as a spectral parameter.
examples.engagement_reason: he invokes six-vertex transfer matrices only as an analogy for commuting operators with a shared spectral parameter

passes.reason: Standard integrable transfer-matrix comparison rather than a structural exception.
It is used only as a standard integrable transfer-matrix analogy, not as a special or adversarial object.

examples.name: String-state contour expansion
examples.note: The inverse Plancherel formula is optionally rewritten in a string-state form familiar from attractive delta-Bose-gas/KPZ replica calculations, showing that several contour descriptions become interchangeable at the lifted q-Hahn level.
examples.engagement_reason: he says the contour formula can be rewritten as a string-state residue expansion, but does not work through the expansion in detail

passes.reason: Residue expansion constrains spectral variables into regular Bethe-string clusters.
The string-state contours arise from residue constraints tying spectral variables into regular bound-state clusters.
the string-state contour expansion is an alternative Plancherel/residue representation rather than a sporadic or adversarial object
the string-state contour formula is a systematic Bethe-residue expansion used for saddle analysis, not a sporadic, limiting, or counterexample case.
The string-state contour expansion uses the regular Bethe-string organization of residues as an equivalent spectral representation.
the string-state contour expansion is a systematic Bethe-residue representation, not a one-off, boundary, or adversarial example

the residue expansion constrains Bethe spectral variables into string or bound-state clusters rather than generic independent points
